title: Micro-Transaction Grinding Attack on Single Merchant (Card Testing Via Small Purchases)
narrative: Fraudster conducts rapid-fire card validation through a single cooperative or compromised merchant using minimal transaction amounts. Over 2-4 hours, 25-60 transactions of $0.50-$3.99 hit the same MID with identical ProductCD (typically 'W' for digital/wallet products). Each transaction uses slight variations in cardholder name or email permutations to evade simple duplicate blocks. The merchant shows unusually high approval rates (92%+ when industry average is 78%) suggesting relaxed fraud controls or merchant complicity. Card BINs span multiple issuers but cluster in specific IIN ranges (431000-432999, 453200-453599) indicating systematic list testing. Transactions occur in tight 45-90 second intervals with card1_txn_count_1hr spiking from 0 to 38+. No CVV failures despite high volume suggests stolen full card data rather than guessed credentials. This grinding phase precedes larger fraudulent purchases once valid cards are confirmed.
indicators: 25-60 transactions to identical MID within 2-4 hour window, all under $4.00
card1_txn_count_1hr increases from 0 to 35+ with no prior merchant history
TransactionAmt consistently between $0.50-$3.99 with 85%+ transactions under $2.00
Approval rate 92%+ versus merchant baseline 75-80% for similar ProductCD
Card BINs cluster in 2-3 narrow IIN ranges (e.g., 4310xx, 4532xx) across different issuer banks
Identical P_emaildomain across 15+ transactions with numerical suffixes (user1@domain, user2@domain)
Zero CVV mismatch flags despite 40+ card presentations suggesting full track data compromise
summary: Rapid-fire micro-transactions ($0.50-$3.99) hitting one merchant in tight intervals, with card counts jumping from zero to 35+ per hour and approval rates exceeding 92%. Card numbers cluster in narrow BIN ranges with no CVV failures, indicating full card data rather than guessing.
reasoning: Fraudsters validate stolen card lists by making tiny purchases that won't alert cardholders, using a merchant with weak controls. Once cards pass this test phase, they're used for high-value fraud elsewhere—the small amounts and tight timing reveal systematic testing rather than legitimate shopping.
indicator_explanations: Merchant sees dozens of tiny purchases from same card in hours
Card goes from zero activity to explosive micro-transaction burst instantly
Every purchase is deliberately kept below typical fraud review thresholds
Approvals are suspiciously higher than normal for this merchant category
Multiple cards share narrow BIN ranges suggesting batch from same source
Email pattern shows automated account generation with sequential numbering scheme
Perfect CVV matches across dozens of attempts indicates full card data
